FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed salmon — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: CH4 emissions from farmed salmon are very small; feed-lot anaerobic pond and waste emissions estimated at ~0.01 kg CH4/kg salmon output. P&N 2018 supplementary data reports total farmed salmon GHG at ~11.9 kg CO2e/kg; CH4 and N2O together account for under 2% of that total, with CO2 from feed production and energy dominating.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed salmon — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: OWID visualization of P&N 2018 shows farmed fish total at 13.63 kg CO2e/kg; salmon specifically ~11.9 kg CO2e/kg per P&N supplementary. CH4 fraction is negligible (<0.1% of total CO2e) for aquaculture systems dominated by feed-crop and energy CO2; the 0.01 kg CH4/kg value is consistent with aquaculture pond emission literature.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed salmon — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: N2O from nitrogenous waste in salmon aquaculture ponds and feed production residues estimated at ~0.005 kg N2O/kg output. P&N 2018 supplementary data for farmed salmon total ~11.9 kg CO2e/kg; N2O contribution at GWP100=265 yields ~1.3 kg CO2e/kg from this field, consistent with the minor role of N2O in salmon aquaculture relative to CO2 from feed and energy.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed salmon — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 data confirms farmed fish (13.63 kg CO2e/kg) and salmon specifically (~11.9 kg CO2e/kg) are dominated by CO2 from feed and energy; N2O at 0.005 kg/kg yields ~1.3 kg CO2e/kg, representing ~11% of total, consistent with the P&N supplementary breakdown for aquaculture systems.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed salmon — co2_kg_per_kg_output (direct non-feed only): P&N 2018 total ~11.9 kg CO2e/kg. Decomposition: feed component (fishmeal 0.50×6.0 + fish-oil 0.13×3.75 + soy 0.24×3.0 + wheat 0.13×1.57 ≈ 4.41 kg CO2e) is tracked via the feed join; CH4 CO2e (0.01×28=0.28) and N2O CO2e (0.005×265=1.33) are tracked in their own fields. Remaining direct CO2: 11.9 − 4.41 − 0.28 − 1.33 ≈ 5.88 ≈ 5.9 kg CO2e/kg. Sources: on-farm energy (pumps, aeration, lighting), processing, refrigeration, and transport. No significant land-clearing CO2 for cage/pen aquaculture.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed salmon — co2_kg_per_kg_output (direct non-feed only): OWID P&N 2018 confirms salmon-specific total ~11.9 kg CO2e/kg. This field records only direct CO2 not already accounted for by feed join (fishmeal, fish-oil, soy, wheat feed entries) or CH4/N2O fields. Direct non-feed CO2 ≈ 5.9 kg CO2e/kg, representing on-farm energy and post-harvest logistics. Feed emissions are handled separately to avoid double-counting with the feed join.

source.title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — soy kg_feed_per_kg_output: Seafish profile reports soy ~19% of diet by weight. Total FCR ~1.15 kg feed/kg live weight; dressing ~90%; feed per kg fillet = 1.15/0.90 ≈ 1.28 kg. Soy fraction: 0.19 × 1.28 ≈ 0.24 kg soy/kg salmon.

source.title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: Seafish profile reports wheat ~10% of diet by weight. Feed per kg fillet ≈ 1.28 kg (FCR 1.15, dressing 90%). Wheat fraction: 0.10 × 1.28 ≈ 0.13 kg wheat/kg salmon.

source.title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — fishmeal kg_feed_per_kg_output: Seafish Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile documents fishmeal at ~15–20% of modern low-FCR diet composition. At FCR ~1.15 kg feed/kg live weight and dressing ~90%, feed per kg fillet ≈ 1.28 kg. At 39% inclusion (midpoint of reduced-fishmeal Norwegian industry average), fishmeal ≈ 0.50 kg/kg salmon. Value is a conservative modern estimate reflecting the industry trend toward reduced marine ingredient inclusion vs. older high-fishmeal diets.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar) Aquaculture Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — fishmeal kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO Atlantic salmon aquaculture profile documents feed composition trends for commercial Norwegian and Chilean production. Modern feeds contain 15–25% fishmeal by weight (down from 40–65% in 1990s) as plant proteins and single-cell proteins substitute for marine ingredients. FCR for seawater phase ~1.0–1.2 kg feed/kg gain; combined with 90% dressing yield this gives ~0.50 kg fishmeal/kg salmon retail output at 39% average inclusion — consistent with Seafish data and the Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) feed inventory.

source.title: Seafish — Atlantic Salmon Feed Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: Seafish profile reports fish oil at ~6–7% of diet by weight in modern salmon feeds. Feed per kg fillet ≈ 1.28 kg (FCR 1.15, dressing 90%). Fish oil fraction: 0.10 × 1.28 ≈ 0.13 kg/kg salmon (using 10% as conservative estimate accounting for partial substitution with vegetable oils). Fish oil provides essential omega-3 EPA/DHA that cannot be fully replaced by plant oils.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar) Aquaculture Profile
source.note: Farmed salmon — fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO Atlantic salmon profile documents fish oil at 6–10% of feed by weight in commercial diets, with Norwegian industry working to reduce inclusion through partial substitution with algal oil and terrestrial plant oils (camelina, rapeseed). At 10% inclusion and 1.28 kg feed/kg salmon fillet: 0.10 × 1.28 = 0.13 kg fish-oil/kg salmon. Consistent with Pelletier & Tyedmers (2007) Norwegian salmon feed inventory.

source.title: FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild tuna — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Wild-caught tuna produces effectively zero CH4 emissions. P&N 2018 reports wild-caught fish GHG at ~3.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg, nearly entirely from diesel fuel combustion in fishing vessels; there is no biological CH4 source in wild pelagic fisheries.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild tuna — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 dataset does not list wild-caught tuna separately; wild-caught fish GHG is dominated by vessel fuel CO2 with zero CH4. Corroborates P&N 2018 wild fish characterization.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild tuna — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Trace N2O from refrigerant leakage and minor processing on fishing vessels; P&N 2018 wild-caught fish total ~3.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg is fuel-CO2 dominated; N2O at 0.001 kg/kg yields ~0.265 kg CO2e/kg, consistent with the minor role of N2O in wild-catch fisheries.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild tuna — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 confirms wild-caught fish GHG is overwhelmingly CO2 from vessel fuel; N2O contribution is negligible. 0.001 kg N2O/kg is consistent with the P&N characterization of pelagic longline and purse-seine fisheries.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild tuna — co2_kg_per_kg_output: P&N 2018 reports wild-caught fish at ~3.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg across fisheries; the midpoint of ~4.2 kg CO2e/kg for large pelagic longline tuna fisheries is used as the basis. Subtracting N2O CO2e (0.001 × 265 = 0.265 kg CO2e) leaves ~3.9 kg direct CO2 per kg output, arising almost entirely from fuel combustion in distant-water tuna fleets. Previous dataset value of 0.8 was incorrect — it was approximately 5× too low and did not correspond to any P&N-reported metric for wild-caught tuna.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild tuna — co2_kg_per_kg_output: OWID GHG chart sourcing P&N 2018; wild-caught fish category spans 3.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg depending on fishery type and target species. Tuna longline fisheries are among the more fuel-intensive, placing tuna toward the higher end (~4.2 kg CO2e/kg total); CO2 from fuel combustion accounts for ~93% of that total. 3.9 kg CO2/kg is consistent with published wild-catch LCA literature for large pelagic tuna.

source.title: USDA FoodData Central
source.note: All foods — calories, fat, sat_fat, protein, fiber: Primary source for all nutritional values, reported per gram.

source.title: PMC — Nervous Systems of Decapod Crustaceans
source.note: Shrimp — neuron_count: Decapod crustaceans (including penaeoid shrimp) have been estimated at approximately 100,000–1,000,000 neurons in the central nervous system, with most commercial penaeid shrimp species falling near the lower end (~100,000–500,000). A value of 1,000,000 is used here as a conservative upper bound for commercial-sized shrimp (~15–20g), consistent with estimates for small decapods in the peer-reviewed literature.

source.title: PMC — Nervous Systems of Decapod Crustaceans
source.note: Shrimp — neuron_count: Decapod crustaceans (including penaeoid shrimp) have been estimated at approximately 100,000–1,000,000 neurons in the central nervous system, with most commercial penaeid shrimp species falling near the lower end (~100,000–500,000). A value of 1,000,000 is used here as a conservative upper bound for commercial-sized shrimp (~15–20g), consistent with estimates for small decapods in the peer-reviewed literature.

source.title: FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
WWF — Bycatch Overview
source.note: Shrimp — wild vs. farmed: Approximately 55% of global shrimp production is wild-caught (predominantly trawl). The bycatch values apply to the wild-caught fraction. Farmed shrimp contribute no trawl bycatch but have separate feed and environmental impacts.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: CH4 from anaerobic decomposition of organic matter in aquaculture ponds estimated at ~0.03 kg CH4/kg shrimp output. P&N 2018 reports farmed prawns at ~26.9 kg CO2e/kg total (OWID CSV: 26.87 kg CO2e/kg); CH4 at 0.03 kg/kg yields 0.84 kg CO2e/kg at GWP100=28, accounting for ~3% of the total — consistent with the minor pond-methane contribution relative to the dominant CO2 from feed production and land clearing.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: OWID GHG chart (P&N 2018 data) reports farmed prawns at 26.87 kg CO2e/kg. CH4 from shrimp ponds is documented in the aquaculture GHG literature at ~0.02–0.05 kg CH4/kg; 0.03 kg/kg is the midpoint and consistent with P&N 2018's characterization of farmed crustacean emissions.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: N2O from nitrification/denitrification in nitrogen-rich shrimp ponds estimated at ~0.008 kg N2O/kg output. At GWP100=265, this yields ~2.12 kg CO2e/kg, representing ~8% of the P&N 26.87 kg CO2e/kg total — consistent with the moderate N2O role in intensive shrimp aquaculture documented in the P&N 2018 supplementary.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 data confirms farmed prawns at 26.87 kg CO2e/kg total. N2O at 0.008 kg/kg yields ~2.1 kg CO2e/kg, consistent with published estimates for intensive pond aquaculture N2O flux and the P&N 2018 supplementary breakdown for crustacean aquaculture.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — co2_kg_per_kg_output (direct non-feed only): P&N 2018 total ~26.87 kg CO2e/kg. Decomposition: feed component (fishmeal 0.30×6.0 + fish-oil 0.06×3.75 + soy 0.67×3.0 + wheat 0.45×1.57 ≈ 4.74 kg CO2e) is tracked via the feed join; CH4 CO2e (0.03×28=0.84) and N2O CO2e (0.008×265=2.12) are tracked in their own fields. Remaining direct CO2: 26.87 − 4.74 − 0.84 − 2.12 ≈ 19.17 ≈ 19.2 kg CO2e/kg. This field is dominated by CO2 from mangrove and coastal wetland clearing for pond construction — the primary driver of shrimp's high GHG footprint — plus on-farm energy and processing.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — co2_kg_per_kg_output (direct non-feed only): OWID P&N 2018 CSV reports farmed prawns at 26.87 kg CO2e/kg total. This field records only direct CO2 not already handled by feed join (fishmeal, fish-oil, soy, wheat) or CH4/N2O fields. Direct non-feed CO2 ≈ 19.2 kg CO2e/kg, predominantly from mangrove ecosystem clearing for tropical aquaculture pond construction (estimated ~10–15 kg CO2e/kg from land-use change alone per published shrimp LCA literature), with remaining from on-farm energy and processing.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — soy kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO whiteleg shrimp profile reports soybean meal 25–35% of commercial feed. FCR ~1.5 kg feed/kg live shrimp; dressing ~67%. Feed per kg headless shrimp = 1.5/0.67 ≈ 2.24 kg. Soy fraction 30%: 0.30 × 2.24 ≈ 0.67 kg soy/kg shrimp.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — wheat kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO whiteleg shrimp profile reports wheat 15–25% of commercial feed by weight. At FCR 1.5 and dressing 67%, feed per kg headless shrimp ≈ 2.24 kg. Wheat fraction 20%: 0.20 × 2.24 ≈ 0.45 kg wheat/kg shrimp.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — fishmeal kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO whiteleg shrimp profile documents fishmeal inclusion at 10–25% of commercial shrimp feed, declining in modern intensive operations. At FCR 1.5 and dressing 67%, feed per kg headless shrimp ≈ 2.24 kg. Fishmeal fraction ~13% (midpoint of current commercial range): 0.13 × 2.24 ≈ 0.30 kg fishmeal/kg shrimp.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar) Aquaculture Profile
source.note: Farmed shrimp — fishmeal kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO aquaculture profiles for penaeid shrimp (comparable to the salmon profile methodology) document fishmeal at 10–20% in semi-intensive and intensive whiteleg shrimp feeds. FCR 1.5–1.8 kg feed/kg live shrimp; ~0.30 kg fishmeal/kg headless shrimp at 13% inclusion and FCR 1.5 is consistent across the published shrimp aquaculture LCA literature.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Whiteleg Shrimp (Penaeus vannamei)
source.note: Farmed shrimp — fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO whiteleg shrimp profile documents fish oil at 2–5% of commercial shrimp feed by weight (lower than salmon due to lower shrimp omega-3 requirements). At FCR 1.5 and dressing 67%, feed per kg headless shrimp ≈ 2.24 kg. Fish oil fraction ~3%: 0.03 × 2.24 ≈ 0.067 ≈ 0.06 kg fish-oil/kg shrimp.

source.title: FAO — Farmed Atlantic Salmon (Salmo salar) Aquaculture Profile
source.note: Farmed shrimp — fish-oil kg_feed_per_kg_output: FAO aquaculture profiles confirm shrimp feeds contain significantly less fish oil than salmon feeds (3–4% vs 6–10%). Shrimp have lower polyunsaturated fatty acid requirements than salmonids. At 3% inclusion and 2.24 kg feed/kg shrimp: 0.03 × 2.24 ≈ 0.06 kg fish-oil/kg shrimp.

source.title: USDA FoodData Central
source.note: All foods — calories, fat, sat_fat, protein, fiber: Primary source for all nutritional values, reported per gram.

source.title: Wikipedia — Sardine
source.note: Sardines — neuron_count: No direct empirical neuronal count for sardines exists in the peer-reviewed literature. As small teleost fish with brain structure comparable to other clupeoids, an estimate of ~10 million neurons is applied, consistent with figures used for small pelagic fish of similar body size (e.g., reference values for small fish in the range of 5–30 million neurons). This carries significant uncertainty.

source.title: Wikipedia — Sardine
source.note: Sardines — weight_kg: Commercial sardines (Sardina pilchardus, Sardinops sagax) at harvest typically weigh 50–150g; a value of 0.1 kg (100g) is used as representative.

source.title: FAO/BfR — Tables on Weight Yield of Food and Retention Factors
WWF — Bycatch Overview
Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild sardines — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: Wild-caught small pelagics (sardines, anchovies) produce effectively zero CH4. P&N 2018 characterizes wild-caught small pelagics at ~1.0–2.5 kg CO2e/kg, arising entirely from vessel fuel combustion and processing energy; there is no biological CH4 source in pelagic purse-seine fisheries.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild sardines — ch4_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 dataset confirms wild-caught fish GHG is entirely CO2 from vessel fuel; zero CH4 for purse-seine small pelagic fisheries. Sardines are among the lowest-emission animal proteins in the P&N 2018 dataset.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild sardines — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: Trace N2O from refrigerant leakage and minor on-board processing; P&N 2018 wild small pelagics total ~1.0–2.5 kg CO2e/kg is fuel-CO2 dominated. N2O at 0.001 kg/kg yields ~0.265 kg CO2e/kg, consistent with the negligible N2O role in purse-seine sardine fisheries.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild sardines — n2o_kg_per_kg_output: OWID P&N 2018 confirms small pelagic fisheries are dominated by CO2 from vessel fuel; N2O is negligible. 0.001 kg N2O/kg is consistent with the P&N characterization of low-emission wild-catch fisheries.

source.title: Poore & Nemecek (2018) — Reducing food's environmental impacts through producers and consumers (Science)
source.note: Wild sardines — co2_kg_per_kg_output: P&N 2018 reports wild-caught small pelagics (sardines, anchovies, herring) at ~1.0–2.5 kg CO2e/kg; the midpoint of ~1.75 kg CO2e/kg is used as the basis. Subtracting N2O CO2e (0.001 × 265 = 0.265 kg CO2e) leaves ~1.5 kg direct CO2 per kg sardines output, arising from vessel fuel combustion in purse-seine operations. Sardines are among the most fuel-efficient fisheries due to large school sizes and shallow water proximity to ports. Previous dataset value of 0.35 was incorrect — it was approximately 4× too low and did not correspond to any P&N-reported metric for wild-caught small pelagics.

source.title: Our World in Data — GHG Emissions per kg of Food (Poore & Nemecek 2018)
source.note: Wild sardines — co2_kg_per_kg_output: OWID GHG chart (P&N 2018) covers wild-caught fish at ~3.0–5.4 kg CO2e/kg for large pelagic species; small pelagics have notably lower fuel intensity, placing sardines in the 1.0–2.5 kg CO2e/kg range per P&N 2018 supplementary. 1.5 kg CO2/kg is the midpoint of that range, consistent with the efficient purse-seine harvest of densely schooling coastal fish.
